en.extract:
The long-wattled umbrellabird (Cephalopterus penduliger) is an umbrellabird in the Cotingidae family. Its Spanish names include pájaro bolsón, pájaro toro, dungali, and vaca del monte. The long-wattled umbrellabird is considered rare and it resides in humid to wet premontane and cloud forest. They are often found on the Pacific slopes of southwest Colombia and western Ecuador, but occasionally are found at lower altitudes.


Description
The male is 40–42 cm in height, with the female being slightly smaller at 35–37 cm. Both sexes are short-tailed and carry an erectile head crest; those of the males are slightly longer at 20–30 cm. The male is distinguished by a large throat wattle of feathers, while females and juveniles have no or a much smaller wattle. The length of the wattle can be controlled, and it can be retracted in flight. The male generally has black colored shafts in its feathers.
The long-wattled umbrellabird's specific name penduliger derives from Latin pendulus, hanging, and refers to the wattle.


Distribution and habitat
The species shows a high level of endemism and is found from the southwestern part of Colombia to the province of El Oro in Ecuador, in the bioregion of Tumbes–Chocó–Magdalena. It inhabits humid montane forests at 1,500-1,800 m above sea level on the ridges and sides of the Andes range.


Ecology


Feeding
The species is mostly frugivorous, and feeds on large fruit. The fruits of the Arecaceae, Lauraceae, and Myrtaceae are preferred. Invertebrates and small vertebrates are also taken.


Reproduction
The long-wattled umbrellabird engages in lek mating, where the males congregate in common areas (leks) for display, which are visited by the solitary females. Females select a male with prominent secondary traits such as aggression and territorial behavior. Nests are built in trees or treeferns, and have been recorded at heights of 4.5–5.0 m above ground.
Clutch sizes in the genus Cephalopterus are generally low; only one egg is laid per nesting attempt. Incubation lasts 27 or 28 days. Only the female incubates and cares for the nestling. She provides food for the nestling on average once per hour and includes invertebrates, vertebrates, and regurgitated material.


Conservation
The species has been classified as vulnerable by the IUCN. A total population of 6,000-15,000 mature individuals was estimated in 2012. It is thought to be under pressure from habitat destruction through deforestation and from hunting. The easy-to-locate lek mating areas make it particularly susceptible to trapping.
The long-wattled umbrellabird is present in several protected areas. The consolidation of existing scattered reserves, as well as the nomination of several existing protected areas as biosphere reserves, could be very beneficial for the species. The curating of forest composition and reforestation have been pointed out as important approaches.


References


External links
A classification of the bird species of South America at Museum.lsu.edu
Birdlife
The Internet Bird Collections
Arkive
EoL

The '''long-wattled umbrellabird''' ('''''Cephalopterus penduliger''''') is an [[umbrellabird]] in the [[Cotingidae]] family. Its Spanish names include ''pájaro bolsón, pájaro toro, dungali'', and ''vaca del monte''.<ref name=":0">{{cite book|last1=Granizo|first1=T|last2=Pacheco|first2=C|last3=Ribadeneira|first3=M.B|last4=Guerrero|first4=M|last5=Suárez|first5=L|title=Libro Rojo de las Aves del Ecuador|date=2002|location=Quito|pages=177–178}}</ref> The long-wattled umbrellabird is considered rare and it resides in humid to wet premontane and [[cloud forest]].<ref name=":5">{{Cite journal|last1=Jahn|first1=Olaf|last2=Grefa|first2=Edwin E. Vargas|last3=Schuchmann|first3=Karl-L.|date=March 1999|title=The life history of the Long-wattled Umbrellabird Cephalopterus penduliger in the Andean foothills of north-west Ecuador: leks, behaviour, ecology and conservation|journal=Bird Conservation International|volume=9|issue=1|pages=81–94|doi=10.1017/s0959270900003373|issn=0959-2709|doi-access=free}}</ref> They are often found on the Pacific slopes of southwest Colombia and western Ecuador, but occasionally are found at lower altitudes.<ref name=":5" />

==Description==
The male is 40–42 cm in height, with the female being slightly smaller at 35–37 cm. Both sexes are short-tailed and carry an erectile head crest; those of the males are slightly longer at 20–30 cm. The male is distinguished by a large throat [[wattle (anatomy)|wattle]] of feathers, while females and juveniles have no or a much smaller wattle. The length of the wattle can be controlled, and it can be retracted in flight.<ref name=":2">{{cite web|last1=Karubian|first1=J.|first2=H. F.|last2=Greeney|first3=J. F.|last3=Freile|year=2010|title=Long-wattled Umbrellabird (''Cephalopterus penduliger'')|website=Neotropical Birds Online|publisher=Cornell Lab of Ornithology|location=Ithaca|url=https://neotropical.birds.cornell.edu/portal/species/overview?p_p_spp=49140}}</ref> The male generally has black colored shafts in its feathers.<ref name=":1">{{cite book|last1=Ridgely|first1=R.|last2=Greenfield|first2=P.|year=2006|title=Aves del Ecuador|location=Quito|publisher=Jocotoco}}</ref>

The long-wattled umbrellabird's specific name ''penduliger'' derives from Latin ''pendulus'', hanging, and refers to the wattle.<ref>{{cite book|last=Granizo|first=T.|year=2009|title=Etimología: nombres científicos de las aves del Ecuador|publisher=Simbioe|location=Quito}}</ref>

==Distribution and habitat==
The species shows a high level of [[endemism]] and is found from the southwestern part of [[Colombia]] to the province of El Oro in [[Ecuador]], in the bioregion of [[Tumbes–Chocó–Magdalena]].<ref name=":0" /> It inhabits humid [[Montane ecosystems|montane forests]] at 1,500-1,800 m above sea level on the ridges and sides of the [[Andes]] range.<ref name=":0" /><ref name=":1"/>

==Ecology==
===Feeding===

The species is mostly [[frugivory|frugivorous]], and feeds on large fruit.<ref>{{cite journal|last=Leck|first=C.|year=1979|title=Avian Extinctions in an Isolated Tropical Wet-Forest Preserve, Ecuador|journal=The Auk|volume=96|issue=2|pages=343–352|doi=10.1093/auk/96.2.343|jstor=4085313}}</ref> The fruits of the [[Arecaceae]], [[Lauraceae]], and [[Myrtaceae]] are preferred. Invertebrates and small vertebrates are also taken.<ref>{{cite book|last=Snow|first=D. W.|year=1982|title=The Cotingas: bellbirds, umbrellabirds, and other species|publisher=Cornell University Presslocation=Ithaca, NY}}</ref><ref>{{cite book|last1=Ridgely|first1=R. S.|last2=Greenfield|first2=P. J.|year=2001|title=The birds of Ecuador|location=Ithaca, NY|publisher=Cornell University Press}}</ref><ref>{{cite journal|last=Berg|first=K. S.|year=2000|title=Field notes on the biology of the Long-wattled Umbrellabird ''Cephalopterus penduliger'' in west Ecuador|journal=Cotinga|volume=14|pages=26–29}}</ref>

===Reproduction===
The long-wattled umbrellabird engages in [[lek mating]], where the males congregate in common areas ([[Lek mating|leks]]) for display, which are visited by the solitary females.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Karubian|first1=J.|last2=Durães|first2=R.|last3=Storey|first3=J. L.|last4=Smith|first4=T. B.|year=2012|title=Mating Behavior Drives Seed Dispersal by the Long-wattled Umbrellabird ''Cephalopterus penduliger''|journal=Biotropica|volume=44|issue=5|pages=689–698|doi=10.1111/j.1744-7429.2012.00859.x|s2cid=56379504 }}</ref> Females select a male with prominent secondary traits such as aggression and territorial behavior.<ref name=":4">{{cite thesis|last=Andrade|first=G.|year=2010|title=Comportamiento y distribución del Pájaro Paraguas Longipéndulo Cephalopterus penduliger en el occidente del Ecuador|publisher=Universidad San Francisco de Quito}}</ref> Nests are built in trees or treeferns, and have been recorded at heights of 4.5–5.0 m above ground.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

Clutch sizes in the genus ''Cephalopterus'' are generally low; only one egg is laid per nesting attempt.<ref>{{cite journal|last1=Karubian|first1=J.|last2=Castañeda|first2=G.|last3=Freile|first3=J.F.|last4=Salazar|first4=R.T.|last5=Santander|first5=T.|last6=Smith|first6=T.B.|year=2003|title=Nesting biology of a female long-wattled umbrellabird ''Cephalopterus penduliger'' in north-western Ecuador|journal=Bird Conservation International|volume=13|issue=4|pages=351–360|doi=10.1017/s0959270903003253|doi-access=free}}</ref> Incubation lasts 27 or 28 days. Only the female incubates and cares for the nestling. She provides food for the nestling on average once per hour and includes invertebrates, vertebrates, and regurgitated material.<ref name=":3">{{cite journal|last1=Greeney|first1=H.|last2=Kirwan|first2=M.|last3=Miller|first3=E.|title=Nesting biology of the Long-wattled Umbrellabird Cephalopterus penduliger. Part II: nestling provisioning|journal=Cotinga|volume=34|year=2012|pages= 96–99}}</ref>

==Conservation==
The species has been classified as [[vulnerable species|vulnerable]] by the [[IUCN]]. A total population of 6,000-15,000 mature individuals was estimated in 2012. It is thought to be under pressure from [[habitat destruction]] through [[deforestation]] and from hunting. The easy-to-locate lek mating areas make it particularly susceptible to trapping.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" />

The long-wattled umbrellabird is present in several protected areas. The consolidation of existing scattered reserves, as well as the nomination of several existing protected areas as [[Man and the Biosphere Programme#Biosphere reserves|biosphere reserves]], could be very beneficial for the species.<ref name="iucn status 12 November 2021" /> The curating of forest composition and [[reforestation]] have been pointed out as important approaches.<ref name=":0" />

zh.extract:
長耳垂傘鳥（Cephalopterus penduliger）是一種分佈在哥倫比亞西部及厄瓜多爾的傘鳥科鸟类。牠們受到失去棲息地的影響，加上體型龐大而容易被獵殺，故現被列為易危。
雄性長耳垂傘鳥頸上有能夠膨脹的肉垂，肉垂長35厘米及由很短的羽毛所覆蓋。雌鳥的肉垂較為細小，約只有雄鳥的一半。
長耳垂傘鳥吃昆蟲及果實。牠們的巢是於2003年被發現的。


參考

Tim Flannery and Peter Schouten. Astonishing Animals: Extraordinary Creatures and the Fantastic Worlds They Inhabit. New York: Atlantic Monthly Press, 2004. Page 26
